content.assessment: Through the whole-system reform lens, the status-quo option is a decision to not alter the structural mechanism generating stratification. The Polish case shows that structural mechanisms (entry-point timing, tier configuration) are what drive measurable outcome shifts; monitoring alone does not constitute a structural mechanism and therefore cannot be expected to alter distributional effects. The Polish reversal experience also demonstrates that structural inertia is not costless: delaying action while problems compound can itself become a policy choice with distributional consequences, mirrored in the sense that Poland's 1999 reform gains were concentrated among previously vocational-track students, implying continued absence of reform forgoes similar gains for the analogous Hungarian population.
opportunities: Could be sequenced as a data-gathering phase preceding a future structural reform, analogous to how Poland's 2022 PISA data only became available after a full post-reversal cohort had passed through the system, suggesting monitoring windows must be long to be informative [F-live-polish_reform-07]
strengths: Avoids the acute implementation costs documented in Poland's 2017 reversal (school closures, teacher displacement, unplaced students) [F-live-polish_reform-05]
Preserves optionality; a highly reversible stance in structural terms since no mechanism is altered
threats: Extended delay risks replicating a 'reform gap' where structural benefits documented elsewhere (Poland 1999) are simply foregone rather than tested locally
weaknesses: Provides no structural mechanism comparable to Poland's 1999 single-structure extension, which is the only lens-relevant precedent showing measurable distributional gains for previously disadvantaged tracks [F-live-polish_reform-04]
Monitoring without structural change cannot be evaluated against the lens's core criterion of structural mechanism, since none is proposed
